Neoplastic cortical thickening rarely with an associated soft tissue mass.

explanation:
Answer C. Periosteal osteosarcoma is an intermediate-grade surface neoplasm. In 68% of cases, calcified spicules of bone extending perpendicular to the cortical margin will be visible radiographically. Characteristically, bone formation is denser near the cortical margin with a soft tissue mass extending from the tumor osteoid. Marrow involvement is rare. Conversely, imaging of parosteal osteosarcomas classically shows a surface soft tissue mass with mature bone extending from the center of the lesion to the periphery with the marrow nearly always involved.

References: Chan CM, Lindsay AD, Spiguel ARV, et al. Periosteal osteosarcoma: a single- institutional study of factors related to oncologic outcomes. Sarcoma. 2018;27:8631237. Yarmish G, Klein MJ, Landa J, et al. Imaging characteristics of primary osteosarcoma: nonconventional subtypes. RadioGraphics. 2010;30(6):1653-1672.

stem: A 70-year-old male presents with a soft tissue mass. On MRI, the lesion is a nonenhancing, well- defined solid mass with smooth margins and homogeneous signal intensity. What is the next best step in this patient's care?

options.text: No further intervention is necessary as the lesion is benign.
6-month follow-up as the lesion is indeterminate.
Percutaneous or surgical biopsy as the lesion is indeterminate.
Ultrasound to characterize fat within the lesion.

explanation:
Answer C. Of the options, the most appropriate next step is biopsy since soft tissue sarcomas may have a bland appearance on MR. Biopsy often allows for a definitive diagnosis. If the lesion does not have a pathognomonic appearance of a specific, benign entity, for instance lipoma, hemangioma, or schwannoma, caution is warranted.

References: Kransdorf MJ, Murphey MD. Imaging of Soft Tissue Tumors. 3rd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2014:46-50. Wu JS, Hochman MG. Soft-tissue tumors and tumorlike lesions: a systematic imaging approach. Radiology. 2009;253:297-316.

stem: A 25-year-old female presents with the history of a neck mass for 2 years. What is the most likely natural history of this tumor? Axial T1 weighted Axial T2-weighted fat saturated Sagittal T2-weighted fat saturated Sagittal T2* gradient echo

options.text: The lesion has stabilized in size since the patient is skeletally mature.
Regression over time with areas of dystrophic calcification and cyst formation.
Invasion of the adjacent neural foramina.
Metastatic disease to the lungs and liver.

explanation:
Answer C. The images show a case of deep fibromatosis (extra-abdominal desmoid). Fibromatoses is a family of soft tissue lesions characterized by benign proliferation of fibrous tissue with varied biologic behavior including infiltrative masses that are locally aggressive. Skeletal maturation has no bearing on their growth potential, and calcification or ossification is rare. Local recurrence ranges from approximately 19% to 77%. Lesions are often debilitating and may be fatal due to invasion of local structures. Wide, local surgical excision, radiotherapy, and chemotherapy have been used as treatments.

References: Kransdorf MJ, Murphey MD. Imaging of Soft Tissue Tumors. 3rd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2014:273-283. Shinagare AB, Ramaiya NH, Jagannathan JP, et al. A to Z of desmoid tumors. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2011;197:W1008-W1014. osteosarcoma) will have pulmonary metastases at presentation? Presence of cortical expansion D. Presence of a sclerotic margin 86 What most accurately describes the cellular origin of soft tissue hemangioendotheliomas? A. Tumor arising from smooth muscle cells of blood vessels B. Tumor arising from collagen fibers in the tunica adventitia of blood vessels C. Tumor of vascular endothelial cells or their precursors D. Tumor of abnormal proliferation of red blood cells 87 A patient presents with a disseminated, hamartomatous disorder composed of hemangiomatous and lymphangiomatous lesions in the axial and appendicular skeletal with visceral organ involvement. What diagnosis is the most accurate? A. Mastocytosis B. Cystic angiomatosis C. Langerhans cell histiocytosis D. Multiple myeloma

stem: Ewing sarcoma characteristically occurs in which part of the bone?
explanation:
Answer C. The majority of cases of Ewing sarcoma are metadiaphyseal in location (44% to 59%), followed by diaphyseal in 33% to 35% and metaphyseal in 5% to 15%. Lesions originating in the epiphysis are rare.

References: Murphey MD, Senchak LT, Mambalam PK, et al. From the radiologic pathology archives: Ewing sarcoma family of tumors: radiologic-pathologic correlation. RadioGraphics. 2013;33:803-831.

stem: What is the most common donor site for cancellous bone grafting?
explanation:
Answer B. The iliac bone is the most frequent site used for bone graft. It allows for a variety of types of bone, mostly cancellous, and for a variety of uses. The anterior iliac crest is the most easily assessed site for grafting.

References: Shin SR, Tornetta P. Donor site morbidity after anterior iliac bone graft harvesting. J Orthop Trauma. 2016;30(6):340-343.

stem: What finding suggests malignant transformation of an osteochondroma to a chondrosarcoma?

options.text: Growth of a previously unchanged osteochondroma in a skeletally immature patient
Thickening of the cartilaginous cap to 1.0 cm
Development of a fluid signal intensity bursa overlying the lesion
Focal lucencies of destruction in the interior of a lesion

explanation:
Answer D. There are multiple factors that may suggest a worrisome appearance for malignant transformation of an osteochondroma to a chondrosarcoma. Growth or increased size, in a skeletally mature patient, of a previously unchanged osteochondroma can be an important indicator of malignant transformation. Focal thickening of the cartilage cap of an osteochondroma to >1.5 cm may suggest malignant transformation. Development of focal destruction with lucent foci within the interior of a lesion is a worrisome sign for malignant transformation of an osteochondroma. Development of an irregular or indistinct surface of the lesion is yet another sign of malignant transformation. Development of a fluid signal intensity bursa overlying an osteochondroma, however, is often related to frictional forces between the lesion and the surrounding soft tissues and is not typically associated with malignant transformation.

References: Alabdullrahman LW, Byerly DW. Osteochondroma. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island, FL: StatPearls Publishing; 2019.

stem: What lesion most commonly occurs in the epiphysis of a long bone?
explanation:
Answer C. Chondroblastomas most commonly occur in the epiphysis or epiphyseal equivalents of bone, and growth may extend into the metaphysis. Unicameral bone cyst, enchondroma, and osteochondromas are benign osseous lesions, which most commonly occur in the metaphysis of bone.

References: Manaster BJ. Diagnostic Imaging: Musculoskeletal: Non-Traumatic Disease. 2nd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Amirsys; 2016:262-265.

stem: Lymphangiomas are benign, primarily soft tissue tumors composed of lymphoid tissue lined by lymphatic endothelium. Where are they most commonly located?
explanation:
Answer C. Lymphangiomas are most commonly located in the head and neck, 75%. Approximately 20% are located in the axilla. 50% to 65% of cases are present at birth with 90% of cases discovered by 2 years of age. Histologic subclassification occurs by vessel composition including capillary, cavernous, cystic, and mixed.

References: Kransdorf MJ, Murphey MD. Imaging of Soft Tissue Tumors. 3rd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2014:200-205.

stem: A 42-year-old male presents with the following radiograph. Pathologic analysis described the lesion as a mixture of histologic elements. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Answer C. Liposclerosing myxofibrous tumor is a benign fibro-osseous lesion composed of a mixture of histologic elements, which may include lipoma, myxoma, myxofibroma, fibroxanthoma, fibrous dysplasia-like features, cyst formation, ischemic ossification, and cartilage. It has a predilection for the intertrochanteric region of the femur. Lesions are often discovered incidentally but may be related to nonspecific pain or, less commonly, pathologic fracture. Differential considerations include fibrous dysplasia and involuting, intraosseous lipoma. Lymphangioma is a developmental, soft tissue lesion of dilated lymphatic channels. Leiomyoma is a benign, slow- growing tumor arising from smooth muscle cells. Langerhans cell histiocytosis is a neoplastic disease primarily of childhood characterized by proliferation of Langerhans cells. Osseous lesions are lytic with radiologic features such as periosteal reaction, endosteal scalloping, cortical breakthrough, and no sclerotic margin.

References: Manaster BJ. Diagnostic Imaging: Musculoskeletal: Non-Traumatic Disease. 2nd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Amirsys; 2016:326-327. Regado ER, Garcia PB, Caruso AC, et al. Liposclerosing myxofibrous tumor: a series of 9 cases and review of the literature. J Orthop. 2016;13(3):136-139.

stem: A 70-year-old man presents with the following radiograph and bone scan. Based on the images, what is the most likely diagnosis of the lesion in the left ilium adjacent to the sacroiliac joint?
explanation:
Answer B. Bone island or enostosis is a focus of compact bone within the cancellous bone. They are most commonly homogeneously dense on radiographs and have a spiculated margin that blends with the adjacent bone. While bone islands are often cold or negative on bone scan, positive findings have been correlated histologically with bone islands. Chondroblastoma is a benign, chondroid tumor most commonly a geographic lytic lesion with sclerotic margin arising in the epiphysis or apophysis of bone. Osteoblastoma are rare, benign bone tumors that may be lytic or contain variable amounts of mineralized matrix. They show intense uptake on bone scan. Multiple myeloma is characterized by lytic osseous lesions.

References: Bernard S, Walker E, Raghavan M. An approach to the evaluation of incidentally identified bone lesions encountered on imaging studies. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2017;208(5):960-970. Ulano A, Bredella MA, Burke P, et al. Distinguishing untreated osteoblastic metastases from enostoses using CT attenuation measurements. AJR Am J Roentgenol. 2016;207(2):362-368.

stem: Which malignancy has the best long-term prognosis?
explanation:
Answer C. The prognosis for parosteal osteosarcoma is better than that for periosteal osteosarcoma, high-grade surface osteosarcoma, conventional osteosarcoma, and telangiectatic osteosarcoma. The 5-year overall survival rate is 86% to 91% for parosteal osteosarcoma, 83% for periosteal osteosarcoma, 67% for telangiectatic osteosarcoma, and 53% to 61% for conventional osteosarcoma. The reported 5-year survival rate for high-grade surface osteosarcoma is 46.1%.

References: Yarmish G, Klein MJ, Landa J, et al. Imaging characteristics of primary osteosarcoma: nonconventional subtypes. RadioGraphics. 2010;30:1653-1672.

stem: What percentage of patients with conventional osteosarcoma (high-grade intramedullary osteosarcoma) will have pulmonary metastases at presentation?
explanation:
Answer A. Osteosarcoma (osteogenic sarcoma) is the most common primary malignant bone tumor in children and adolescents. Presence of pulmonary metastatic disease at the time of diagnosis ranges from 5% to 15% of cases. Most cases occur in patients in their second and third decades with a slight male predominance. The vast majority of osteosarcomas arise in the metaphysis of long bones. Of all of the variants of osteosarcoma, conventional osteosarcoma accounts for approximately 75% of cases.

References: Kaste SC, Pratt CB, Cain AM, et al. Metastases detected at the time of diagnosis of primary pediatric extremity osteosarcoma at diagnosis: imaging features. Cancer. 1999;86:1602-1608. Manaster BJ. Diagnostic Imaging: Musculoskeletal: Non-Traumatic Disease. 2nd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Amirsys; 2016:214-219. Murphey MD, Robbin MR, McRae GA, et al. The many faces of osteosarcoma. RadioGraphics. 1997;17(5):1205-1231.

stem: What malignant fibrous histiocytoma of bone radiographic feature is the most accurate?
explanation:
Answer A. Malignant fibrous histiocytoma (MFH) is a pleomorphic sarcoma that occurs most commonly in soft tissues, although it may also occur primarily in bone. MFH of bone accounts for approximately 2% to 5% of all primary malignant bone tumors with a peak prevalence in the fourth decade of life. Most tumors arise in the appendicular skeleton, predominantly centered in the metaphysis of long tubular bones. Although most cases of MFH arise spontaneously, tumors can also develop in the setting of prior radiation, surgery, trauma, or preexisting benign osseous lesions. In contrast to other primary tumors of bone, MFH is not typically associated with periosteal reaction. It generally does not have a sclerotic margin and does have an associated soft tissue mass, findings consistent with malignancy. It typically does not have cortical expansion.

References: Manaster BJ. Diagnostic Imaging: Musculoskeletal: Non-Traumatic Disease. 2nd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Amirsys; 2016:328-331. Murphey MD, Gross TM, Rosenthal HG. From the archives of the AFIP: Musculoskeletal malignant fibrous histiocytoma: radiologic-pathologic correlation. RadioGraphics. 1994;14(4):807-826.

stem: What most accurately describes the cellular origin of soft tissue hemangioendotheliomas?

options.text: Tumor arising from smooth muscle cells of blood vessels
Tumor arising from collagen fibers in the tunica adventitia of blood vessels
Tumor of vascular endothelial cells or their precursors
Tumor of abnormal proliferation of red blood cells

explanation:
Answer C. Hemangioendotheliomas, along with Kaposi sarcoma and angiosarcoma, are vascular sarcomas arising from endothelial cells or their precursors. Hemangioendotheliomas are generally less aggressive than angiosarcomas. Leiomyosarcoma is a tumor arising from smooth muscle cells of blood vessels. Polycythemia vera is a myeloproliferative disorder of uncontrolled red blood cell production.

References: Cioffi A, Reichert S, Antonescu CR, et al. Angiosarcomas and other sarcomas of endothelial origin. Hematol Oncol Clin N Am. 2013;27:975-988. Kransdorf MJ, Murphey MD. Imaging of Soft Tissue Tumors. 3rd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2014:215-220. Manaster BJ. Diagnostic Imaging: Musculoskeletal: Non-Traumatic Disease. 2nd ed. Philadelphia, PA: Amirsys; 2016:584-585.

stem: A patient presents with a disseminated, hamartomatous disorder composed of hemangiomatous and lymphangiomatous lesions in the axial and appendicular skeletal with visceral organ involvement. What diagnosis is the most accurate?
explanation:
Answer B. The condition described is cystic angiomatosis. Radiographically, the lesions are characteristically round or ovoid, well defined, and lytic with sclerotic margins with no matrix formation or periosteal reaction. Visceral organ involvement is present in 60% to 70% of cases.

References: Dos Anjos CS, Borges RMC, Chaves AC, et al. Cystic angiomatosis, pleural effusion and multiple bone lesions mimicking a metastatic malignant neoplasia: a case report. J Med Case Rep. 2019;13(1):265. Najm A, Soltner-Neel E, Le Goff B, et al. Cystic angiomatosis, a heterogeneous condition: Four new cases and a literature review. Medicine (Baltimore). 2016;95(43):e5213.
